characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: An air-headed cyborg police inspector who's rather clueless. Accidental Hero:There are a number of times when Gadget is legitimately helpful and contributes towards solving the case. It's just that he tends to do it purely by accident.Gadget:[whenever he's congratulated for solving the case]I did? There are a number of times when Gadget is legitimately helpful and contributes towards solving the case. It's just that he tends to do it purely by accident. Adaptational Badass: Several of the video game tie-ins (particularlythe SNES gameandInspector Gadget: Mad Robots Invasionfor thePlayStation2) tone down his usual bumbling in favor of saving the day by himself with little to no assistance from Penny or Brain due to having the player control him throughout the game and his gadgets function consistently in line with the game's mechanics as well as Penny and/or Brain either having their presence downplayed or getting incapacitated and kidnapped as part of the game's plot. In the case of the Super Nintendo game, his rise in effectiveness is justified by the fact that the game's objective is to rescue Penny after Dr. Claw kidnaps her and there were several instances in the source material where the knowledge that Penny's in danger made Gadget become more competent and efficient in fighting the bad guys. Adaptation Dye-Job: InInspector Gadget's Last CaseandGadget and the Gadgetinis, Gadget's trench coat and hat are considerably darker shades of gray with his gloves being yellow, although the trench coat is also a notably different style to the original.

characters.name: Augustin Tamare/Inspector Gadget
characters.description: An air-headed cyborg police inspector who's rather clueless. Accidental Hero:There are a number of times when Gadget is legitimately helpful and contributes towards solving the case. It's just that he tends to do it purely by accident.Gadget:[whenever he's congratulated for solving the case]I did? There are a number of times when Gadget is legitimately helpful and contributes towards solving the case. It's just that he tends to do it purely by accident. Adaptational Badass: Several of the video game tie-ins (particularlythe SNES gameandInspector Gadget: Mad Robots Invasionfor thePlayStation2) tone down his usual bumbling in favor of saving the day by himself with little to no assistance from Penny or Brain due to having the player control him throughout the game and his gadgets function consistently in line with the game's mechanics as well as Penny and/or Brain either having their presence downplayed or getting incapacitated and kidnapped as part of the game's plot. In the case of the Super Nintendo game, his rise in effectiveness is justified by the fact that the game's objective is to rescue Penny after Dr. Claw kidnaps her and there were several instances in the source material where the knowledge that Penny's in danger made Gadget become more competent and efficient in fighting the bad guys. Adaptation Dye-Job: InInspector Gadget's Last CaseandGadget and the Gadgetinis, Gadget's trench coat and hat are considerably darker shades of gray with his gloves being yellow, although the trench coat is also a notably different style to the original.

characters.name: Penny
characters.description: Age-Inappropriate Dress: At least three times, she's dressed in a very revealing bikini despite being a child. Age Lift: Penny is a teenager in most of the sequel cartoons. Badass Adorable: A young girl who succeeds in anonymously helping her uncle thwart Dr. Claw's plans. Badass Bookworm: She has a book that's also a computer (the best of both worlds). She's also suggested to be a very good student. Borrowed Catch Phrase: She sometimes uses her uncle'sCatchphraseof "wowsers!" She has also occasionally used Gadget's "Go-Go Gadget" phrase when using her computer book to activate one of the Gadgetmobile's gadgets.

characters.name: Brain
characters.description: Penny's super-intelligent dog, who always assists her when she works in the shadow to help Gadget. As Penny is not anAction Girland always needs to use her portable computer, it's Brain who always does the field work and ensures Gadget doesn't get killed. Action Pet: Although more wacky than outright action, he still goes through things that one would have to be tough to get though. Beleaguered Assistant: Zigzagged. Sometimes he's the one who gets injured while he's saving Gadget. Other times he's clear of whatever happens with the M.A.D. agents taking the fall instead. A few rare occasions haveSwapped Roleswhere Gadget is the one saving him, often when Gadget thinks he's a M.A.D. agent trying to flee, but he's really about to fall off a cliff or a waterfall. Butt-Monkey: Trying to protect Gadget often gets Brain into even more trouble, doubly so when Gadget inevitably mistakes him for a M.A.D. agent. Cartoon Dog Breed: He's a big yellow dog, with white ear tips, of indeterminate breed. The live-action films made him a beagle.

characters.name: Chief Quimby
characters.description: Big Good: He's Gadget's boss and the chief of the organization employing him. Butt-Monkey: Every time Gadget receives a self-destructing message, Quimby will inevitably get stuck with the note exploding in his face. Catchphrase:"Great job, Gadget! You've done it again!" (used when he shows up at the end of the episode to arrest the MAD agents);"Why do I put up with him?" Used in several episodes after the assignment message blows up in his face."GADGET!!!" "Great job, Gadget! You've done it again!" (used when he shows up at the end of the episode to arrest the MAD agents); "Why do I put up with him?" Used in several episodes after the assignment message blows up in his face.

characters.name: Corporal Capeman
characters.description: Alliterative Name: His name is an alliteration of the letter C. Adapted Out: No effort was made to incorporate him into the continuity of the live-action films. Chuck Cunningham Syndrome: Other than the direct-to-videoCompilation MovieGadget's Greatest Gadgetsmentioning him in theFraming Deviceand featuring two of the episodes of the original series he appeared in ("The Capeman Cometh" and "Gadget's Gadgets"), he hasn't appeared in any media since the second season of the original cartoon, to the extent that the two direct-to-video filmsInspector Gadget's Last CaseandInspector Gadget's Biggest Caper Everas well as theSequel SeriesGadget and the Gadgetinisnever made any effort to acknowledge him or explain where he's been since Gadget's glory days. Fat Idiot: He is rather portly and is even dumber than Gadget. In-Series Nickname: For some reason, Penny often addresses him as "Capey".

characters.name: Gadgetmobile
characters.description: Brought to You by the Letter "S": Typically, the letter "G" is somewhere on the exterior of the car. Cool Car: In the 1980s series, the Gadgetmobile is a van that can transform into an interceptor mode that resembles.... well, a lot of 1980s sports cars, like the Lotus Esprit, Toyota Supra, theDeLorean, or (befitting the show's French origins) theMatra Murena. In the live-action films, it's a 1960s Lincoln Continental. Ret-Canon: His personality and features from the 1999 film are so liked that they appear in the direct-to-DVD filmsInspector Gadget's Last CaseandInspector Gadget's Biggest Caper Ever. Crosses over withCanon Immigrant, considering the Gadgetmobile wasn't a character in its own right in the original cartoon. Sentient Vehicle: He is depicted as a sentient vehicle in the direct-to-DVD filmsInspector Gadget's Last CaseandInspector Gadget's Biggest Caper Ever. Suddenly Speaking: After being made a speaking sentient vehicle in the 1999 film, it carried over to the car's depictions in the direct-to-DVD filmsInspector Gadget's Last CaseandInspector Gadget's Biggest Caper Ever.

characters.name: Professor Von Slickstein
characters.description: Absent-Minded Professor: He's clearly a brilliant scientist but also a bit of a ditz. Adapted Out: He doesn't appear in either of the live-action Disney films, which respectively replace him with Dr. Brenda Bradford and Baxter. Bungling Inventor: When kidnapped and ordered to build killer robots for Dr Claw, he builds them to look like Gadget and programs them with his own personality, making them act like doddering old men and thus useless. While this could have been deliberate sabotage on his part, his puzzled reaction to Dr Claw's outrage indicates he really thinks there is nothing wrong with them. Dub Name Change: In the French version he's called "Professeur Rotoscope" in his first appearance, but he's called "Slickstein" like in English in all his subsequent appearances. Einstein Hair: He looks like a stereotypicalMad Scientistdown to the hair.

characters.name: Fidget and Digit
characters.description: Identical Twin ID Tag: They are physically identical except that Fidget is orange and Digit is blue. Mini-Me: They share Gadget's gadgets and facial features but only half of his height. Ridiculously Human Robot: They are fully electronic, with no human parts, yet have plenty of human emotions, no matter how much they may argue about it. Those Two Guys: They are a pair of silly sidekicks who get into a lot of arguments about how sentient their creator Penny succeeded in making them and how incompetent theirClueless Detectivesuperior is. Sequel Non-Entity: No work made afterGadget and the Gadgetinishas used them.

characters.name: Loony Purkle
characters.description: Cannot Spit It Out: He has a hard time letting Penny know he wants to go on a date with her. Lovable Coward: He isn't ashamed to scream when surprised but is a kind-hearted servant of justice. Remember the New Guy?: He has a close rapport with Gadget and Penny and has worked against evil for a while but only appears inInspector Gadgets Biggest Caper Ever. The Smart Guy: He is aGadgeteer Geniushelping the Inspector with electrical inventions and repairs.

characters.name: Colonel Nozzaire
characters.description: Cultured Warrior: He is an accomplished military man (albeit oneKicked Upstairsto a desk job) who enjoys stuff like vintage planes, glass figurines, gardening, and towers made of playing cards, most of which Gadget ends up destroying. French Jerk: He is a French man prone to egotism, seething rage, and joy at the idea of Gadget dying.Jerk with a Heart of Gold: He can show concern for Gadget on occasion (being quick to believe Penny when she argues he was framed for treason in "Prison Cruise"), bonds with Brain over their shared suffering, and makes an effort toTurn the Other Cheekto his misfortunes in some episodes. Jerk with a Heart of Gold: He can show concern for Gadget on occasion (being quick to believe Penny when she argues he was framed for treason in "Prison Cruise"), bonds with Brain over their shared suffering, and makes an effort toTurn the Other Cheekto his misfortunes in some episodes. Sequel Non-Entity: No work made afterGadget and the Gadgetinishas used him.

characters.name: Dr. Claw
characters.description: Ambiguously Human: With a voice like that... Though, several occasions show his body clearly is human. Angry Fist-Shake: He often shakes his fist after every defeat. Animal Motifs: Cats. As well as having his right hand cat, the insignia of his evil corporation is a stylized cat face, which Claw can be seen wearing as a ring, as well as his vehicles being styled after it. Arch-Enemy: Gadget and Claw consider each other to be personal enemies. Bad Guys Play Pool: He enjoys pool and has been known to play a round with one of his agents on occasion.

characters.name: MAD Cat
characters.description: Butt-Monkey: Can occasionally be this to some ofDr. Claw'stantrums. Cats Are Mean:Supposedly.He doesn't really do anything evil, though, and in many cases he actually comes off as more of a victim to Dr. Claw's outbursts. Evil Laugh: Has a great one made of cat hisses. It's pretty muchthe only way you can tellthat he's a bad guy. Gender Flip: Is female in the 2015 series. The Pirates Who Don't Do Anything: He's a villain, but does little aside from lounging around while Dr. Claw either pets him or takes his anger over failed plans out on him. Averted on a few rare occasions when he pushes a button on Dr. Claw's computer or on the MAD Mobile's control panel.

characters.name: The Great Wambini and the Lesser Wambini
characters.description: Karma Houdini: Are the first of the second season's recurring M.A.D. Agents that avoid arrest. Meaningful Name: The Great Wambini is a highly skilled magician while his lackey the Lesser Wambini is only capable of lame and underwhelming tricks. Stage Magician: Their usual disguise.

characters.name: Professor Dummkopf
characters.description: Beard of Evil: He has a big brown shaggy beard. Herr Doktor: He has the accent, and his name is even a German word. Karma Houdini: He never gets arrested in any of his appearances. Mad Scientist: In his three episodes, he has tried to use a shrink ray on Gadget as well as sending a quintet of imps and a hulking brute he created himself to kill him. Paper-Thin Disguise: He often uses one in the episodes featuring him.

characters.name: Dr. Spectrum
characters.description: Bald of Evil: He has no hair. Karma Houdini: Yet another recurring M.A.D. Agent who never gets caught. Knight of Cerebus: Unlike most of the mad scientist M.A.D. Agents on the show, he has very few humorous traits and "Bad Dreams are Made of This", one of the episodes featuring him, isDarker and Edgieras it involves everyone in Metro City being tormented by bad dreams and even has Brain experiencing a nightmare where it is implied that M.A.D. Cat killed him. Mad Scientist: He's invented tablets that become "ghosts", a device that enables people to teleport through telephones, and a machine that makes people suffer nightmares.

characters.name: Dr. Null and Dr. Void
characters.description: Creepy Twins: They are both virtually identical. Karma Houdini: They never get captured in any of the three episodes featuring them. Verbal Tic: In their first appearance, they would often say the exact same phrase, but with differing usage of words. They reprise this in their third and final appearance.

characters.name: Thelma Botkin
characters.description: Bad Guys Play Pool: Like Dr. Claw, she enjoys pool and is playing a round with him toward the beginning of "Gadget's Clean Sweep". Karma Houdini: She is seen arrested at the end of the first episode with her, but the next has her free and back with M.A.D. with no explanation. She does not get arrested in her next two appearances. Time Travel: Her episodes had her attempting to go back in time and kill Gadget's ancestors.

characters.name: Les Renown
characters.description: Greater-Scope Villain: He is Dr. Claw's mentor and therefore is indirectly responsible for the existence of Claw's crime organization M.A.D. Karma Houdini: The final recurring "villain" of Season 2 who is never arrested, although whether or not he really is a Houdini is arguable since he isn't really seen committing any crimes other than conspiracy (he introduces Dr. Claw to other elderly criminals who do get arrested at the end of their respective three episodes). Old Master: At least Dr. Claw seems to regard him as one; Claw claims Renown mentored him in the criminal arts. Punny Name: His name is a pun on "less renowned", referring to how Dr. Claw is more well known than he is.
